# Concept Card: On-Prem

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

- Content: So if you kind of go through the CRI control, uh, utility and try and create containers with it, then eventually Kubelet is going to delete them because the Kubelet is unaware of, uh, some of those containers or pods that are created outside of its knowledge.

### Evidence 495: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E93
- Type: Concept

- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 496: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E94
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Cgroups is the Linux feature, the Linux primitive that allows whether you're working with Docker or Kubernetes, it allows you to specify resource limits on your containers.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 497: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E95
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So if you want to say this container can only use 512MB of Ram, it's using the Cgroups under the hood.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 498: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E96
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, when you configure the Kubelet process and when you configure the the runtime, which in our case is container D, there's two different drivers that are available.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 499: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E97
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You have cgroup FS which is the default, and then you have system D.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 500: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E98
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now you can use whichever one you'd like, but you have to make sure that whichever one you select, both the Kubelet and your container runtime are configured to use the same Cgroup driver.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 501: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E99
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So if the Kubelet is set to Cgroup fs, then the container runtime has to be set to the same thing.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 502: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E100

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now if we keep reading, you can see there's a section here that says when system D is chosen as the init system for your Linux distribution, you want to make sure that you set the Cgroup driver to be system D.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 946: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And hence Docker became the most dominant container tool.

### Evidence 947: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then came Kubernetes to orchestrate Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 948: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Kubernetes was built to orchestrate Docker specifically in the beginning.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 949: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Docker and Kubernetes were tightly coupled and back then Kubernetes only worked with Docker and didn't support any other container solutions.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 950: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then Kubernetes grew in popularity as a container orchestrator.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 951: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And now other container runtimes like rocket wanted in Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 952: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Users needed it to work with container runtimes that are other than just Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 953: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so Kubernetes introduced an interface called Container Runtime Interface or Cry.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 954: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So cry allowed any vendor to work as a container runtime for Kubernetes, as long as they adhere to the OCI standards.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 955: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So OCI stands for Open Container Initiative, and it consists of an image spec and a runtime spec.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 956: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Image spec means the specifications on how an image should be built.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 957: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that's what it defined.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 958: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: An image spec defined the specifications on how an image should be built, and the runtime spec defined the standards on how any container runtime should be developed.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 959: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So keeping these standards in mind, Anyone can build a container runtime that can be used by anybody to work with Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 960: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that was the idea.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 961: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So rocket and other container runtimes that adhere to the OCI standards were now supported as container runtimes for Kubernetes via the CRI.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 962: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, Docker wasn't built to support the CRI standards because remember, Docker was built way before CRI was introduced and Docker still was the dominant container tool used by most.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 963: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Kubernetes had to continue to support Docker as well.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 964: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E28
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so Kubernetes introduced what is known as Docker shim, which was a hacky but temporary way to continue to support Docker outside of the container runtime interface.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 965: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E29
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: While most other container runtimes worked through the CRI, Docker continued to work without it.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 966: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E30
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now you see Docker isn't just a container runtime alone.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 967: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Docker consists of multiple tools that are put together, for example Docker CLI, the Docker API, the build tools that help in building images.
- Multi-concept tags: CKA-CKAD, On-Prem

### Evidence 968: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There was support for volumes or security.

### Evidence 969: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And finally also the container runtime called run.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 970: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: See the daemon that managed run C.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 971: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that's that was called as container D.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 972: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So container D is CRE compatible and can work directly with Kubernetes as all other runtimes.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 973: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So container D can be used as a runtime on its own separate from Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 974: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E38
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So now you have container D as a separate runtime and Docker separately.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 975: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E39
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Kubernetes continued to maintain support for Docker Engine directly.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 976: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E40
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, having to maintain the Docker shim was an unnecessary effort and added complications, so it was decided in version 1.2 for release of Kubernetes to remove Dockershim completely, and so support for Docker was removed.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 977: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E41
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt

### Evidence 978: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E42

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So all the Docker images continue to work because Docker followed the image spec from the OCI standard.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 979: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E43
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 980: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E44
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So they continue to work with Containerd.

### Evidence 981: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E45
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But Docker itself was removed as a supported runtime from Kubernetes.

### Evidence 982: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E46
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 983: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E47

### Evidence 984: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E48

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt

- Content: So container D although is part of Docker, is a separate project on its own now and is a member of CNCF with the graduated status.

### Evidence 985: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E49

### Evidence 987: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E51

### Evidence 1212: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/005_Containers Overview.extraction.md::E77
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 005_Containers Overview.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The overhead causes higher utilization of underlying resources, as there are multiple virtual operating systems and kernels, running the virtual machines also consume higher disk space, as each VM is heavy and is usually in gigabytes in size, whereas Docker containers are lightweight and are usually in megabytes in size.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt

### Evidence 1225: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt

- Content: But Docker's user experience made working with containers super simple, and hence Docker became the most dominant container tool.

### Evidence 1226: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then came Kubernetes to orchestrate Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1227: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Kubernetes was built to orchestrate Docker specifically in the beginning.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1228: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Docker and Kubernetes were tightly coupled and back then Kubernetes only worked with Docker and didn't support any other container solutions.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1229: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then Kubernetes grew in popularity as a container orchestrator.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1230: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And now other container runtimes like rocket wanted in Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1231: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Users needed it to work with container runtimes that are other than just Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1232: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so Kubernetes introduced an interface called Container Runtime Interface or CRE.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1233: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So CRE allowed any vendor to work as a container runtime for Kubernetes as long as they adhere to the OCI standards.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1234: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So OCI stands for Open Container Initiative, and it consists of an image spec and a runtime spec.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1235: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Image spec means the specifications on how an image should be built.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1236: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that's what it defined.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1237: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: An image spec defined the specifications on how an image should be built, and the runtime spec defined the standards on how any container runtime should be developed.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1238: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So keeping these standards in mind, Anyone can build a container runtime that can be used by anybody to work with Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1239: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that was the idea.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1240: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So rocket and other container runtimes that adhere to the OCI standards were now supported as container runtimes for Kubernetes via the CRI.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1241: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, Docker wasn't built to support the CRI standards because remember, Docker was built way before CRI was introduced and Docker still was the dominant container tool used by most.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1242: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Kubernetes had to continue to support Docker as well.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1243: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so Kubernetes introduced what is known as Docker shim, which was a hacky but temporary way to continue to support Docker outside of the container runtime interface.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1244: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E28
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: While most other container runtimes worked through the CRI, Docker continued to work without it.
- Multi-concept tags: On-Prem

### Evidence 1245: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E29
- Type: Operational Insight
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now you see Docker isn't just a container runtime alone.

### Evidence 1389: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Architecture
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 03_Kubernetes Concepts / 012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Earlier, we talked about the different components of Kubernetes that make up a control plane and worker nodes such as the API server, etcd key value store controllers and scheduler on the master or the control plane, and then you have the Kubernetes and container runtime on the worker nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 1390: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 03_Kubernetes Concepts / 012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, it would take a lot of time and effort to set up and install all of these various components on different systems, individually, by ourselves, which is why a mini cube bundles all of these different components into a single image, providing us a pre-configured single node Kubernetes cluster so we can get started in a matter of minutes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, On-Prem

- Content: Now, keep in mind, if you're running on a mac that has only eight gigs of Ram, you will see that the script that you run will actually only deploy a single server, and that's just due to the memory limitations of your machine.

### Evidence 1858: learn-kubernetes/11_Appendix - Setup Multi Node cluster using Kubeadm/060_Demo - Setting Up Virtual Machines on Mac.extraction.md::E11

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With eight gigs, it doesn't give us a lot of flexibility to spin up multiple VMs, so the script will automatically detect that you have eight gigs only, and it'll only deploy a single node for you, and then you're basically going to deploy Kubernetes onto a single node instead of doing it on three different nodes.
